content.text: A capability gap closure plan converts the maturity assessment baseline into funded, time-bound action. Without a closure plan, the baseline report becomes an artefact that confirms what everyone suspected but changes nothing. The closure plan is the bridge between diagnostic finding and operational investment — it names what will be done, by whom, in what order, with what budget, and how success will be measured.

attrs.alt: Capability gap closure dimension progression planning

content.text: From Gap to Plan: The Prioritisation Principle
Not all gaps can be closed in parallel, and not all gaps matter equally. The prioritisation principle is straightforward: close the gaps that are binding constraints on programme objectives first.
Priority Tier
Gap Type
Closure Approach
Tier 1 — Blocking
Gap prevents a committed programme objective from being met in Year 1
Immediate investment; dedicated owner; monthly progress check
Tier 2 — Constraining
Gap will become a blocking constraint in Year 2 unless addressed now
Q2–Q3 investment; included in annual plan; quarterly check
Tier 3 — Improvement
Gap reduces efficiency or quality but does not block objectives
Opportunistic: addressed when budget allows or as part of a larger initiative
Tier 4 — Aspirational
Gap between current state and 'Leading' (Level 5) on non-critical dimensions
Deferred to Year 2+ planning; noted in the maturity report

Tier 1 gaps have a named owner and a funded action before the baseline report leaves the ESC meeting. Tier 2 gaps have a funding commitment before the quarter closes. Tiers 3 and 4 are tracked but not actively funded in Year 1.
Dimension-Level Closure Plans
Each dimension gets its own closure plan that specifies the sequence of actions needed to advance from the current level to the target level. Actions are sized to be completable within a quarter.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal

@dataclass
class ClosureAction:
    action: str
    owner: str
    quarter: str        # e.g. "Q1-2026"
    investment_usd: int
    expected_level_gain: float
    success_metric: str

@dataclass
class DimensionPlan:
    dimension: str
    current_level: int
    target_level: int
    actions: list[ClosureAction] = field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def gap(self) -> int:
        return self.target_level - self.current_level

    @property
    def total_investment(self) -> int:
        return sum(a.investment_usd for a in self.actions)

    def projected_level(self) -> float:
        return self.current_level + sum(a.expected_level_gain for a in self.actions)

    def summary(self) -> str:
        proj = self.projected_level()
        return (
            f"{self.dimension}: L{self.current_level} -> L{self.target_level} "
            f"(projected L{proj:.1f}) | ${self.total_investment:,} | "
            f"{len(self.actions)} actions"
        )

# Example: Talent & Culture dimension plan (gap = 2 levels)
talent_plan = DimensionPlan(
    dimension="talent_culture",
    current_level=2,
    target_level=4,
    actions=[
        ClosureAction(
            action="Hire 2 senior ML engineers with LLM specialisation",
            owner="CoE Lead + CHRO",
            quarter="Q1-2026",
            investment_usd=380_000,
            expected_level_gain=0.5,
            success_metric="Both hires onboarded and contributing to sprint by Q1 end",
        ),
        ClosureAction(
            action="Launch AI literacy programme for all senior managers (half-day workshop series)",
            owner="L&D Lead",
            quarter="Q2-2026",
            investment_usd=45_000,
            expected_level_gain=0.3,
            success_metric="80% completion rate across senior manager cohort",
        ),
        ClosureAction(
            action="Establish BU AI guild network with named guild leads in each BU",
            owner="CoE Lead",
            quarter="Q2-2026",
            investment_usd=20_000,
            expected_level_gain=0.4,
            success_metric="5 BU guilds active; monthly cross-guild forum running",
        ),
        ClosureAction(
            action="Implement AI engineer career track with defined levels and promotion criteria",
            owner="CHRO",
            quarter="Q3-2026",
            investment_usd=15_000,
            expected_level_gain=0.5,
            success_metric="Career framework published; first cohort of promotions processed",
        ),
        ClosureAction(
            action="Embed AI leads in top-3 revenue BUs",
            owner="CoE Lead + BU GMs",
            quarter="Q4-2026",
            investment_usd=290_000,
            expected_level_gain=0.3,
            success_metric="3 embedded AI leads reporting to both CoE and BU; OKRs signed",
        ),
    ],
)
print(talent_plan.summary())
# talent_culture: L2 -> L4 (projected L4.0) | $750,000 | 5 actions
Closure Plan Actions by Dimension
The action types that move each dimension vary significantly. Common closure levers per dimension:
Dimension
Level 1→2 Actions
Level 2→3 Actions
Level 3→4 Actions
Strategy & Vision
Document informal AI strategy; get exec sign-off
Align AI roadmap to business planning cycle; establish ESC
Integrate AI KPIs into corporate scorecard; portfolio governance
Data & Infrastructure
Identify priority datasets; assign data owners
Deploy basic data catalogue; implement model registry
Feature store; automated quality gates; full LLMOps pipeline
Talent & Culture
Identify AI champion in each BU; ad hoc training
Hire CoE core team; launch AI guild; executive literacy
Embedded BU AI leads; formal career track; learning culture metrics
Risk & Governance
Assign AI risk owner; document known risks
Publish AI risk policy; add bias testing to deployment checklist
Automated monitoring; red-teaming; regulatory mapping
Delivery & Ops
Define deployment process; add basic monitoring
CI/CD for models; uptime SLAs; on-call rota
Automated rollback; canary deployment; latency SLAs per model
Value & Measurement
Define one success metric per use case
Business KPI linkage; quarterly value reviews
Causal attribution; A/B testing; portfolio ROI dashboard
Sequencing and Budget Allocation
When total gap closure investment exceeds the annual budget (which it almost always does), sequencing decisions must be explicit rather than left to whichever team shouts loudest.

Budget allocation follows the prioritisation tiers. Tier 1 gaps receive full funding before any Tier 2 budget is released. Within a tier, gaps are sequenced by the combination of gap size and cost-effectiveness (expected level gain per dollar invested).
Investment Sizing Guidelines
Level Advance
Typical Investment Range
Primary Cost Drivers
L1 → L2
Leadership time, basic tooling, first hire
L2 → L3
Platform build, team expansion, training programme
L3 → L4
Advanced tooling, embedded talent, process automation
L4 → L5
R&D investment, external advisory, competitive differentiation

These are order-of-magnitude estimates for a 1,000–10,000 employee organisation. Regulated industries (financial services, healthcare) typically run 1.5–2x these figures due to compliance overhead. Start-up-mode teams with high AI talent density can run at 0.5x.
The 90-Day Closure Sprint

The 90-day sprint is the first concrete execution cycle after the baseline is accepted. It is not a project plan — it is a focused sprint on the top five actions needed to advance the two or three Tier 1 gaps. The sprint structure:
Week
Activity
Output
Sprint kickoff: owners confirm capacity, resources confirmed, success metrics agreed
Sprint charter signed by CoE Lead and ESC sponsor
Execution: weekly status check (15 min per action owner)
Weekly one-line status per action: on track / at risk / blocked
Mid-sprint review: identify any actions that will not complete by Day 90
Revised plan for at-risk actions; ESC notified of any blocking issues
Sprint close: measure outcomes against success metrics
Closure report: actions completed, level advances achieved, next sprint scope

Actions that are not completed within 90 days are either rescoped (broken into smaller pieces that can complete) or escalated to the ESC as blocking issues. An action that remains open across three consecutive sprints is a signal that it is either under-resourced or incorrectly specified.
Sprint Governance

The sprint is owned by the PMO and reviewed by the CoE Lead, not the ESC. The ESC receives a one-page summary at the end of each sprint — not weekly status. This keeps the ESC focused on programme-level decisions rather than sprint management.
Action owner
— Named individual accountable for delivery. Not a team. Not a department.
Sprint master
— PMO lead who facilitates the weekly check-in and escalates blocked actions.
CoE Lead
— Reviews sprint status weekly; resolves resource conflicts; signs off the closure report.
ESC
— Receives end-of-sprint report; authorises next sprint scope if budget adjustment needed.
Tracking Maturity Progression

Maturity progression is tracked at two levels: action-level (is the specific action completed?) and dimension-level (has the expected level advance materialised?). These are not the same. An action can complete without advancing the maturity level if the action was misspecified.
Tracking Artefact
Cadence
Owner
Audience
Action status dashboard
Weekly
PMO
CoE Lead, action owners
Sprint closure report
End of each 90-day sprint
PMO
CoE Lead, ESC
Dimension score delta (mini re-assessment)
End of each 90-day sprint
CoE Lead + independent reviewer
ESC
Annual maturity re-assessment
12 months after baseline
CoE Lead + independent reviewer
ESC, board

The dimension score delta is a lightweight check: for each dimension where a closure action completed in the sprint, the independent reviewer confirms whether the evidence now supports the next level score. This prevents score advancement being claimed on the basis of action completion alone — the maturity rubric, not the action checklist, is the arbiter.
Build vs. Buy vs. Partner Decisions in Gap Closure
For each dimension gap, the closure team must decide whether to build the capability internally, buy it from a vendor, or acquire it through a partnership. The decision matrix differs by dimension:
Dimension Gap
Build Signals
Buy Signals
Partner Signals
Data & Infrastructure
Unique data architecture; proprietary data assets; competitive moat
Commodity platform need (vector DB, model gateway, MLOps tooling)
Cloud provider integration; specialist data platform vendor
Talent & Culture
Long-term strategic capability; core IP development; 12+ month runway
Short-term skill gap; niche specialisation (e.g. EU AI Act compliance attorney)
University partnerships for talent pipeline; guild community of practice
Risk & Governance
Risk policy ownership is always internal; control design must be owned
Compliance tooling; bias testing frameworks; audit tooling
Law firm for regulatory interpretation; external red-team for adversarial testing
Delivery & Ops
Platform is differentiated; high volume of custom deployments
Standard CI/CD; monitoring platforms; incident management tooling
Systems integrator for implementation acceleration

The default for commodity capabilities is always Buy — reinventing MLOps tooling when Weights & Biases, MLflow, or equivalent platforms exist wastes engineering capacity that should be applied to differentiated problems. Build is reserved for capabilities where the organisation's specific context makes a general solution unfit.
Closure Plan Risk Register
Every closure plan carries execution risks. Documenting them upfront prevents them from becoming surprises mid-sprint:
Risk
Likelihood
Impact
Mitigation
Key hire takes 4+ months (talent market tight)
High
Delays L2→L3 advance by one quarter
Start hiring in Q1 even if budget not fully confirmed; use contractor bridge
Data catalogue vendor delays integration
Medium
Blocks data_infrastructure advance to L3
Parallel track: document catalogue manually while vendor integration runs
BU GM withdraws guild lead nomination
Medium
Stalls talent_culture advance
Secure written commitment from GM in sprint charter; ESC sponsor backup available
Budget cut mid-year reduces Tier 2 investment
Low
Tier 3 gaps deferred; Tier 2 slip 1 quarter
Tier 2 plans pre-approved with clear cut-line; sponsor aware
Independent reviewer capacity conflict
Medium
Mini re-assessment delayed
Book reviewer slots 6 weeks in advance; identify backup reviewer
Communicating Progress to the ESC
The ESC does not need sprint-level detail — it needs to know whether the maturity trajectory is on track. The standard ESC progress update for gap closure covers four items on one page:
Dimension delta
— Table showing baseline score, current validated score, and target score for each dimension. Updated at each 90-day sprint close.
Tier 1 action status
— RAG status for each Tier 1 action. Green = on track; Amber = at risk, mitigation in place; Red = blocked, ESC action needed.
Investment vs. plan
— Actual spend vs. approved closure budget to date. Variance > 15% triggers ESC discussion.
Decisions needed
— Any items requiring ESC decision before the next sprint can proceed (usually additional budget release or resource allocation conflicts).

A maturity progression update that shows all dimensions advancing at plan is suspicious — closure plans almost always encounter friction. An update that shows one dimension lagging with a named root cause and a revised plan is credible and actionable.
Multi-Year Progression Planning

The 90-day sprint addresses Tier 1 gaps. Multi-year progression planning addresses the full trajectory from baseline to a sustained capability level — typically a 3-year horizon for a mid-size enterprise programme:
Year
Typical Focus
Expected Overall Level
Governance Milestone
Year 1
Close Tier 1 gaps; establish foundational platform and talent
2.5–3.5 (org. average)
ESC constituted; charter signed; 90-day sprints running
Year 2
Close Tier 2 gaps; advance lagging dimensions; embed in BUs
First annual re-assessment; peer benchmark completed; board reporting established
Year 3
Optimise high-performing dimensions; achieve Level 4 on all priority dimensions
AI integrated into corporate strategy planning; potential external thought leadership

Year 3 plans are directional rather than operational — the actions and investments at Year 3 depend on where Year 2 leaves the organisation, which cannot be fully predicted at programme start. Set Year 3 level targets, then define specific actions in the Year 2 annual planning cycle.
Common Failure Modes in Gap Closure
Action without owner
— Closing a gap owned by 'the team' produces no progress. Every action must have a named individual.
Plan without budget
— A closure plan that has not been approved as a budget line by the time the sprint starts will stall when invoices arrive.
Actions that don't advance the rubric
— Completing a training course does not advance Talent & Culture if the rubric evidence requirement is an active guild network. Map each action explicitly to the rubric criterion it satisfies.
Equal priority to all gaps
— Distributing effort evenly across all six dimensions advances all of them slowly. Concentrating on the two binding constraints advances them quickly.
Score advance claimed on completion, not evidence
— Declaring Level 3 because the policy was published, before checking whether the policy is operationally followed, produces score inflation.
Summary
Gap closure plans prioritise by tier: Tier 1 (blocking) gets immediate funded investment; Tier 2 (constraining) gets Q2–Q3 investment; Tiers 3–4 are tracked but not actively funded in Year 1.
Each dimension plan specifies concrete actions, named owners, quarters, budget, expected level gain, and success metric — not aspirational statements.
The 90-day sprint is the primary execution unit: five actions, two or three Tier 1 dimensions, weekly check-ins, end-of-sprint maturity delta check.
Budget allocation follows gap size and cost-effectiveness; total Year 1 gap closure investment typically runs $500k–$2M for a mid-size enterprise depending on current maturity and target level.
Maturity progression is validated against the rubric criteria, not against action completion — a policy published without operational evidence of use does not advance the score.
